Appendices

About the Appendices
Economic theory, and in particular the theory that makes up this volume,is mathe
maticalin character. Formal definitions that make use of conceptsfrom mathematics
(sets, binary relations, functions) are followed by lemmas, propositions, and corol
laries. Your ability to make it through this book depends crucially on your level
of comfort with the various pieces of mathematics that are used and in particular
with your level of comfort with theorem-proof exposition.
     These appendices present two types of material. In some cases, they review
mathematical concepts, tools, and results that I hope and expect you have learned
elsewhere. I assume that most readers of this book are graduate students (or per
haps advanced undergraduates)in economics and related fields, who have a solid
background in mathematics in general and in the specific types of mathematics
(analysis, probability) that are employed in particular. If your background is both
solid and recent, you will find yourself able to read through many of the appendices
with little problem.
    But in some cases, and this is particularly true of Appendix 4(on the mathe
matics of correspondences)and Appendix 6(on dynamic programming),it is likely
that much of the material will be new to you.
     In either case, these appendices are not substitutes for good courses on the
subjects they cover. They do not,for the most part, provide problem sets for you to
do, and what is true for economic theory is even more true for mathematics: You
learn the subject not so much by reading about it as by doing it and, in particular,
by doing the problem sets. (Where problems are provided, you should take ad
vantage and do them,especially if the material is new to you.) If you find yourself
struggling with the material in any appendix, or if the material is entirely new to
you,I urge you to find and consume a good textbook that covers the material thor
oughly. I asked colleagues and economics Ph.D. students at the Stanford Graduate
School of Business for textbooks that they would recommend for such study, and I
was surprised to find that books that I studied as a student(a long, long time ago)
are still regarded as good sources: Kudin's Principles of Mathematical Analysis (3rd
ed.), McGraw-Hill, 1976; Royden, Real Analysis (3rd ed.), Prentice-Hall, 1988; and
Rockafellar, Convex Analysis, Princeton University Press, 1970. One more recent
book that was strongly recommended by some students and that covers a wide va
riety of subjects is Ok,Real Analysis with Economic Applications, Princeton University
Press, 2007. Concerning dynamic programming, Bertsekas, Dynamic Programming
and Optimal Control, vols. 1 and 2(3rd edition), Athena Scientific, 2005 and 2007,
was recommended. I personally like Whittle, Optimization Over Time: Dynamic Pro-
438                                                              About the Appendices

gramming and Stochastic Control, vols. 1 and 2. But this is out of print,I fear. Perhaps
copies can be found in your library.
     I have not included in the appendices mathematics that is truly basic. In partic
ular, basic(multivariate)calculus,linear algebra, set theory, and simple probability
theory are for the most part omitted.
     When writing the appendices, I struggled with the question. Which results
should I prove and which should I simply state? For the most part, I omit proofs
where the proof is straightforward, and I omit proofs where the proof is so difficult
that giving the proof would derail the exposition. ButI do give proofs,or sometimes
sketches of proofs, whenever I think your comprehension of the result will benefit
by going through the details.
Appendix One

Mathematical Induction


Mathematical induction (or just, induction)is a technique for proving that a given
statement is true for any positive (or nonnegative) integer. For those readers who
are unfamiliar with this very useful technique, or who need a quick refresher, I will
illustrate with a very silly example; For my statement, I will use Every nonnegative
integer n is either even or odd, where n is even if n = 2m for some integer m,and n is odd
if n = 2m + 1 for some m. (You probably think, and correctly, this is petty obvious.
But did you ever think how such a thing is proved? Here is one way.)

Step 1: First, you prove the statement is true for n = 0 if you want to prove it is true
for all nonnegative integers, or you prove it is true for n = 1, if you want to prove
it is true for all strictly positive integers.
      In this case, we'll begin with n = 0. Zero is even, since 0 = 2x0.
Step 2: The induction step. Second, you assume or hypothesize that the statement is
true for all nonnegafive/positive integers less than or equal to n, and, using this
hypothesis, you prove the statement is true for n + 1. The assumption is called the
induction hypothesis, and this step is called the induction step.
     So assume inductively that every nonnegative integer n or less is either even
or odd. Then, in particular, either n = 2m (n is even) or n = 2m + 1 (n is odd).
Consider n+1. If n is even,so n = 2m for some m,then n+1 = 2m+l,hence n+1 is
odd. If n is odd,so n = 2m+l for some m,fhen n+1 = 2m+l+l = 2m+2 = 2(m+l),
and n + 1 is even. Therefore, we have shown thaf n + 1 is either even or odd,
completing the induction step.
Step 3; Write end of proof; you are done.

    You should have a pretty good intuitive understanding of why this is a valid
technique of logical proof. Basically, this is a "domino" technique. The positive
integers are the dominos, and domino n is considered to be knocked over if the
statement is true for integer n. In step 1 you prove that the first domino is knocked
over. In step 2, you prove that if all the dominos up to n are knocked over, then so
is n + 1. And then, because the integers are like an infinite sequence of dominos,
this shows that the statement is true for every integer; every domino is knocked
down.

    Suppose I want to prove some statement is true for all the integers, positive
and negative. I can use the technique of induction by,for example,showing that the
statement is true for zero, and then showing that if it is true for all positive integers
440                                              Appendix One: Mathematical Induction

from 0 to n > 0, it is true for n + 1, while if it is true for all negative integers from
0 down to to m < 0, then it is true for m - 1.
      Although it is probably overdoing things, let me close by noting that this
involves in particular the idea that there is always a "next" integer. Statements
about all the real numbers are not amenable to this technique. For example,suppose
I wanted to prove that All nonnegative real numbers are rational. I could carry out, in
each case, step 1: Zero is rational. But then the logic in step 2 is that I assume that
the statement is true for all the numbers less than or equal to n and prove it is true
for the "next" number. There is no next real number after 0,so the proof technique
doesn't work.
Appendix Two

Some Simple Real Analysis
This appendix reviews basic concepts and results from real analysis that are used in
the text. As mentioned in the overall introduction to the appendices,if you are able
to read through this appendix at a healthy clip, with most of the material coming
back to you from some previous course you took, you are ready to proceed to the
text and most problems. If it is familiar, but only vaguely so, you may need to review
seriously. And if your have never seen this material before, it may be dangerous
to rely on this as your sole resource; study a good textbook on real analysis. The
overall introduction to the appendices gives some recommendations.


A2.1. The Setting
The explicit setting is finite-dimensional Euclidean space, which is fancy math-
talk for the space of A:-dimensional vectors with real numbers for components,for
some positive integer k. Most of what is discussed here extends to real vector
spaces and even to various metric and topological spaces, but I do not comment
further on those extensions. Euclidean space of dimension k is denoted by i?*,
with X = (xi,... jXfc) denoting a typical element of       . The positive orthant is
denoted R!1; that is, a: e     if a; > 0. The strict positive orthant, vectors with
all components strictly positive, is denoted          The letters f, g, and h usually
denote functions on subsets of R^ with range R^,for some(possibly other)integer
j. The function / is called real valued if its range is R. (that is, R^).

      Open and Closed Sets

between the vector x ~ y = {x^ — y^,... ,Xk - yk) and 0 = (0,...0); distance is

  1- ||a^ — y\\ > 0, and ||x — y| = 0 if and only if x = y.

Definition A2.3. A set X C    is open iffor every x e X, there exists some e > 0
such that N^{x) C X. Aset X C R is closed if it is the complement ofan open set. Per
careful application ofthese definitions, both the empty set and the entire space R^ are open
and, hence, both are closed.

The picture is a simple one: A set is open if, around every point, you can draw
some "ball" of postive radius that is completely contained within the original set.^
The mental picture of a closed set will be developed momentarily. Many sets in i?''
are neither open nor closed; for instance, in R^, the set (0,1] = {x £ i? : 0 < x < 1}
is neither.

 Proposition A2.4. The union of any number of open sets is open. The intersection of a
finite number ofopen sets is open. The union of afinite number of closed sets is closed, and
 the intersection ofany number of closed sets is closed.^

Relatively open and closed sets
In many contexts, we talk about functions defined not on all of i?'"' but instead on
a subset of R'^ or conduct analysis in such a subset. Perhaps most importantly, we
often look at functions defined on the positive orthant of . To deal with such

 ' A mathematician would say,            is a normed linear space, and the distance between x and y is
the norm of their difference.

   ^ Exercise: Show that for e > 0, iVe(x) is an open set. Hint: Use the triangle inequality.
   ^ If all this is brand new to you, you should go through the proof of these things. Proving the first
should be easy, and the second only a bit harder. For the two statements about closed sets, you must
learn De Morgan's laws: The complement of a union of sets is the intersection of their complements,
the complement of an intersection of sets is the union of their complements,(You can even prove this, if
you are feeling fanatical.) You should also develop or be shown the standard examples of a (non-finite)
intersection of open sets that is not open.
A2.2. Distance, Neighborhoods, and Open and Closed Sets                             443



cases, we say that a subset F of a set X is relatively open in X if F = F n X for
some set Z that is open in all of     and F is relatively closed if F = Z n X for
some set Z that is closed in all of f?''. Nofe that if X is closed, then F is relatively
closed in X if and only if it is closed, and if X is open,then F is relatively open in
X if and only if it is open. When conducting analysis in some particular domain,
authors will sometimes drop the modifier relatively and speak of open and closed
sets, implicitly meaning relatively open and relatively closed.
Closure and interior

Definition A2.5. For X C i?'', the closure of X is the smallest closed subset of
containing X,and the interior of X is the largest open subset of R’^ contained within X.
How do we know that for a given set X,there is a smallest closed set containing
X and a largest open set contained in X ? We use Proposition A2.4: To define the
closure of X,note that R’^ is closed and contains X. Now take the intersection of
every closed set that contains X. Proposition A2.4 tells us that this is necessarily
a closed set. Of course, X is entirely contained in this intersection (since X is in
each set in the intersection). And this must be the smallest closed set containing
X; anything smaller would have been in the intersection, and hence would have
reduced the intersection to itself(at least). Similarly, the interior of X is the union
of all open sets entirely contained within X. Note that the interior of X can be
empty.
    Please note as well that if x is in the interior of X, then there is some e > 0
such that N^{x) C X. In words, there is a ball around x contained in X. Why?
Because if x is in the interior of X,since the interior of X is open,there is an open
ball around x in the interior of X, which (perforce) is entirely contained within
X. Conversely, if for a; 6 X we can find an e > 0 such fhat N^ix) C X, we are
certain that x is in the interior of X: The inferior of X is fhe union of all open sets
contained within X, and Nfx) is one such open set. Hence the interior of X is
equivalently defined as the set of points in X such that we can put some open ball
around x and stay entirely within X. (A corresponding equivalent definition of
closure follows in a few pages.)
     If you desire some finger exercises on this material,a good one at this point is to
show that the closure of a set X is the complement of the interior of its complement,
and vice versa.
     Note that the relative interior of a sef X, relative to another set F, defined
as the largest relatively open set (relative to F)contained in X is not, in general,
the relative interior of X intersected with F;for instance, the relative interior of
X relative to itself is itself. On the other hand, the relative closure of X,relative
to F, which is the smallest relatively closed set containing X,is the closure of X
intersected with F (prove this!).
Other measures of distance
The Euclidean distance between x and y, given above, can be mathematically
cumbersome to work with. There are many other ways to measure distance in R'^,
444                                                    Appendix Two: Some Simple Real Analysis

two of which can be more tractable in proofs. These two are based on the so-called
sup or £°° norm (read i°° as "ell-infinity")and the sum-of-absolute-values or (} (read
"ell-one") norm. In the first, the distance between x and y is

                       X - y\\   OC   = max {Ixj - 2/il : i = l,...,fc};

in the second, the distance between x and y is

                                                     k

                                      X
                                           -y||i =         -ViV
                                                     i=\




(In this parlance. Euclidean distance is based on the f norm, and there is an (P
norm for every p from 1 to oo.) Thinking in terms of E?,||a; - y||i is the distance
from a: to y if you can only move north-south or east-west at a time; it counts the
number of "city blocks" that must be walked to getfrom a: to y,if the city is laid out
like Manhattan; jja: — y| (without a subscript, meaning Euclidean distance) is the
distance from x to y as the crow flies; and ||a: - y||oo is the larger of the north-south
or east-west distance between x and y.
      Hence if a; = (1,3) and y = (5,0), ||a; - y||i =4 + 3 = 7,           X —   y\\ = V¥Ty =
\/25 = 5, and ||a; - y||oo = max{4,3} = 4. In fact, it is generally true that

                           \ x-yh > l|a;-?/ll > l|a;-y|            oo




for all X and y. But it is also true that

                                                      1
                                      ,x   -ylloo > T\\x-y\\
                                                    k
                                                             ll




where k is the dimension of the Euclidean space. For this reason, when it comes
to defining open and closed sets, the three different ways of measuring distance
are equivalent. If a set is open with respect to one way of measuring distance, it is
open with respect to the other two. This last statement is not meant to be obvious,
although it is not hard to prove. And this has the following consequence: Many
of the concepts we are about to define—and in particular, continuity of functions
and boundedness and compactness of sets—don't depend on which of these mea
sures of distance are used. (In mathematical terms, continuity and compactness
are topological properties, and since the different ways of measuring distance don't
change which sets are open,they induce the same topologies.) Therefore, in the text,
when I want to prove that some function is continuous or a some set is bounded
or (after Appendix 4)some correspondence is locally bounded, I can and will use
whichever of the three distance measures is most convenient mathematically. If
you have never studied these ideas in detail before, when this happens I will be
pulling the proverbial rabbit out of a hat; I hope this dicussion will at least indicate
the nature of the rabbit and the hat, although to tranform the argument from magic
A2.3. Sequences and Limits                                                                         445



to transparent logic, you must study the issue rather more deeply than is possible
in this appendix.


A2.3. Sequences and Limits
A sequence in  is, formally, a function from the positive integers {1,2,3,...} to
  . Being somewhat less formal, it is a countably infinite list of points                          1 • •


from R^. The connection between the formal and informal definitions is that x* is
the value of the function at the argument i. We write              as shorthand for a
sequence, and {a.-*} as short shorthand.'*
     Note the use of superscripts here; we are reserving subscripts for components
of the vector,so that(for example) a* will mean the jth component of the i th vector
in the sequence {,t®}. In math books, subscripts are most often used for sequence
indices; indeed,in several places in the text we revert to this practice (armouncing
the fact when this happens).

Definition A2.6. The sequence {x*} has limit x iffor every e > 0 there is some integer
I such thatfor all i > I,||x* - x| < e. When this happens, we write limi^ao      = x,
limx’- = X, or x* ^ x.

In words, the sequence has limit x if the points in the sequence get and stay arbi
trarily close to X.

Proposition A2.7. The set X C R^ is open if and only if,for every point x € X and
sequence {x®} with limit x, there is some large enough integer I such that x® G A for all
i > I.


Proposition A2.8. The set X C R^ is closed ifand only if,for every sequence {x®} with
limit X, if x‘‘ € X for all i, then x e X.

Proposition A2.8 gives the most useful mental picture of closedness of a set; X is
closed means that whenever we take a sequence from X with a limit, that limit is
also in A. In other words, A contains all its limit points.
     The phrase “whenever we take a sequence from A with a limit..." implicitly
makes the important point that some sequences do not have a limit. For example,in
    the sequence {x®} where x® = lOf—the sequence {10,20,30,...}—has no limit.
A more intriguing example is the sequence {1.1,2.1,1.01,2.01,1.001,2.001,...}.
This also has no limit. It might be said that both 1 and 2 are limits of this sequence,
but thatis not quite right,since the sequence gets arbitrarily close to both,arbitrarily
late in the sequence, but does not stay arbitrarily close to either. The points 1 and
2 are not limits of this sequence;instead they are its accumulation points:

   ^ This use of curly brackets to set off a sequence may cause confusion,since curly brackets are also
used to delineate a set. Suppose,for instance,for a sequence {x*}, we want to indicate the countable set
of values taken on by the sequence. Following standard notational usage, this would be {x*;i = 1,...}.
The first means an ordered sequence of points; the second an unordered countable set of points. As
long as you are aware of the distinction, the context should indicate which is meant.
446                                            Appendix Two; Some Simple Real Analysis

Definition A2.9. For {x^} a sequence in        , the point e R’^ is an accumulation
point ofthe sequence iffor every e > 0 andfor every positive integer I, there is some i > I
such that||x® — a:| < e.

In words, the sequence has x as an accumulation point if arbitrarily late in the
sequence, we get arbitrarily close to the point, even if we don't stay close forever
after. That is, we return to every neighborhood of x over and over again, but we
might leave that neighborhood repeatedly as well.
Definition A2.10. For a sequence {x*}, a subsequence of this sequence is a sequence
{y^} where y® = x^'for some infinite increasing sequence of integers {nj,n2,..•}.
If the definition confuses you, two simple examples should clarify matters: For a
given sequence {x®},two subsequences are {x^, x‘*, x®,...} and {x', x^“, x™,x^™ ...}.
The point of this new terminology is to recast the definition of an accumulation
point: X is an accumulation point of {x®} if and only if there is some subsequence
{x®®®} of {x®} that has x as its limit. (For more finger exercises, prove that these
two definitions are equivalent.)
     Proposition A2.8 is generalized in two steps. First, x is an accumulation point of
the set X C R^ if there is some sequence {x®}, where x® e A for all i, such that x
is an accumulation point of {x®}. (In view of the previous paragraph, this in turn
is equivalent to requiring that x is the limit of {x®}, for some (possibly different)
sequence drawn from X.) And then: A set A C R^ is closed if and only if it
contains all its accumulation points.
     In fact, if we take any set A—open, closed, or neither—and we add to A
all of the accumulation points of A, we get the closure of the set A. (Another
exercise: Definition A2.5 says that the closure of a set A is the smallest closed
set containing A, the intersection of all closed sets containing A. Prove that an
equivalent characterization ofthe closure of A is A and all its accumulation points.)

A2.4. Boundedness,(Completeness),and Compactness
Consider the sequences {10,20,30,...} and {1.1,2.1,1.01,2.01,1.001,2.001,...}.
The second has accumulation points 1 and 2, while the first has no accumulation
points at all. The first has no accumulation points, apparently, because the numbers
"blow up." In fact, this is the only way a sequence from R^ could fail to have an
accumulation point.

Definition A2.11. A subset X of i?'® is bounded if there exists some M >0 such that
||x| < M for all x e X. Equivalently, the set is bounded if there is some M' > 0 such
that,for all x = (xi,... ,Xfc) e A, \xf < M'.
Proposition A2.12. Every bounded sequence has at least one accumulation point.
(A sequence is bounded if the set of its points is a bounded set; that is, if for some
large-enough scalar M,||x®| < M for all i.) Proposition A2.12 depends on very
basic and deep properties of the real numbers connected to their construction and,
in particular, to the property of completeness, which is defined in two steps:
A2.5. Continuous Functions                                                                         447



  1. A sequence {x"} from               is called a Cauchy sequence if for every e > 0, there
                                           m
     exists an N such that \\x' — X             < e for all n and m,both > N.

  2. The space R!^ is complete, meaning that every Cauchy sequence {x"-} from
     R^ has a limit in R^.

If you are feeling very adventurous mathematically, prove Proposition A2.12 under
the assumption that i?'' is complete.
Definition A2.13. A subset X of R^ is compact if it is dosed and bounded.
Corollary A2.14 to Propositions A2.8 and A2.12. If {x”} is a sequence drawnfrom a
compact set X,then {x”} has one or more accumulation points, all of which lie in X.

I won't bother making it a formal proposition or corollary, but please note that
every finite subset of R,’^ is compact. (If this is new to you, prove it.)
     In most math books you encounter, A2.13 is not the definition of compactness.
Instead, the definition runs: A set X is compact if it has the property that every
open cover of X —a collection of open sets           7 G C} such that X lies within the
union of the Z.y —admits a finite subcover, or X is a subset of the union of finitely
many of the Z.y. The Heine-Borel Theorem shows that for Euclidean spaces(among
others), this is equivalent to so-called sequential compactness, which is essentially
the conclusion of the corollary, and then the definition given here (A2.13)is shown
to be an equivalent definition to the more primitive definitions. But in terms of
workaday economic theory, the characterization given in Definition A2.13 is most
often used to verify that a set is compact, with the conclusion of the corollary the
normal harvest, a harvest that is then turned into any number of other results. In
this regard, it may be worth cross-referencing the answer to Problem 1.12 given
in the Student's Guide, which uses the first and usually most primitive definition
of compactness to show directly, without recourse to Debreu's Theorem, that for
continuous preferences, (A) is nonempty for nonempty and compact sets A.

A2.7. Suprema and Infima; Maxes and Mins
Definition A2.19.

  a. Aset X C        is bounded above ifthere is some x £R^ such that x > x for all x £ X.
      X is unbounded above if it is not bounded above. Similar terms and definitions are
      used for iun)bounded below,
  b. For any set X C R that is bounded above, the supremum of X,denoted sup X,is
     the smallest z £ R such that z > x for every x £ X. If X is unbounded above, we
     write sup X = oo.
if there exists some K > 0 such that for every e > 0, 5   Ke "works"; and it is uniformly Lipschitz
continuous on A if A can be picked independently of x.
A2.8. The Maximum of a Continuous Function on a Compact sSet                                 449



  c. For any set X C R that is bounded below, the infimum of X,denoted inf X,is the
     largest number z £ R such that z < x for every x G X. If the set X is unbounded
     below, we write inf X = -oo.
 d. For a set X C R,when sup X G X,we write max X for this value, calling this the
    maximum in (or of) X. When sup X ^ X,we say that the maximum in X does
    not exist. Similarly, when inf X G X,we write min X for this value, calling it the
    minimum in (or of) X.

Part b of the definition implicitly assumes that any bounded-above set of real num
bers has a finite supremum; part c makes a similar assumption about bounded-
below sets and their infima. These things are true and are part of the deep structure
of the real numbers, cormected (as with Cauchy sequences)to the fact that the real
numbers are complete. Although it will cause mathematicians to wince(because it
is stating as a proposition something that is really an axiom of the real numbers), I
state this result in the form of a proposition.

Proposition A2.20.
  a. If X is a nonempty subset of R, then infX < supX.
  b. Every nonempty subset X C R that is bounded above has a finite supremum and
     every subset X C R that is bounded below has afinite infimum.
  c. Every nonempty compact subset of R, has a maximum and a minimum.

Note in this proposition that X is assumed to be nonempty. We will encounter
constructions in which the supremum and /or infimum of a set in R is taken, where
the set(in some cases)is empty. Itis conventionalto regard the infimum ofthe empty
set as oo and the supremum of the empty set as -oo. In several propositions to
follow, you'll find the formulation "for every nonempty and compact set X." In
most cases that you find this formulation, the "nonempty" part is there to guard
against suprema that are (1) -oo and (2) therefore, not in X.


A2.8. The Maximum of a Continuous Function
         on a Compact Set

utility function. Because it is the climax, I give two proofs, the second of which is

Therefore, as long as we produce an upper semi-continuous utility function, we
know utility maximization leads to a choice on every (nonempty) compact set of
alternatives.

Appendix Three

Convexity
Notions of convexity—^both convexity of sets and of functions—play an extraordi
narily important role in microeconomic theory.
     As in Appendix 2, the setting is finite-dimensional Euclidean space, . All
the functions we consider are from subsets (nearly always convex subsets) X of
    to the real line R. A crucial notion, which does not rate a formal definition,
is a convex combination of two points: If x and y are from R’^ and a is a scalar (a
real number) between 0 and 1, the point ax + {l - a)y is a convex combination of
X and y. The set of all such points, fixing x and y and allowing a to vary from 0
to 1, is the set of convex combinations of x and y. Geometrically, this is the line
segment that joins x and y. Strict convex combinations are points ax +{l — a)y for
a G (0,1), the open interval from 0 to 1, as long as x =/= y; geometrically this is the
line segment less its endpoints.


A3.1. Convex Sets

Definition A3.1. Aset X C R^ is convex if,for every pair of points x and y from X
and a e [0,1], ax + {l — a)y £ X. In words, every convex combination ofevery pair of
pointsfrom X is in X.

This rules out sets with holes, sets with borders that "bend in," and sets that are
composed of several unconnected pieces. In Figure A3.1a,I draw a typical convex
set, while the sets in A3,lb, c, and d illustrate three ways convexity can fail.




      (a) A convex set     (b) Not convex,    (c) Not convex,     (d) Not convex,
                            because of the    because of the      because it has
                                 hole           indentation         two pieces

                         Figure A3.1. Convex and nonconvex sets


    By a strict reading of the definition (or, if you prefer, by convention),the empty
set is a convex set.
A3.1. Convex Sets                                                                      453



     It is perhaps overly pedantic, but let me state as a formal proposition the
following:
Proposition A3.2. If X is a convex set, {a^;£ = 1,.. . , nj is a set of n nonnegative
scalars such that     = l,and {x^-,£ = 1,...,n} are n points selectedfrom X,then
e:           is also an element of X.
In words, convex combinations of finitely many elements from X are in X,if A is
a convex set. This is proven by a standard induction argument from the definition
of convexity (where n = 2), inducting on the number n. Another completely
straightforward result is:
Proposition A3.3. The intersection ofany number ofconvex sets is convex.
    In Appendix 2, we discussed the notion of the closure of any set, the smallest
closed set containing the original set. In similar fashion, we have:
Definition A3.4. The convex hull of a set X         , denoted CH(X) is the smallest
convex set containing X. The closed convex hull of a set X Q ,denoted CH(X) is
the smallest closed-and-convex set containing X.
Propositions A3.3 and A2.4 together guarantee the existence of CH(X) and CH(X)
for any X: As in the definition of the closure, we simply take the intersection of all
convex or closed-and-convex sets that contain X,noting that R^ is one such.
    We have defined CH(X) as the smallest convex set containing X or, equiv
alently, as the intersection of all convex sets that contain X. But there is another
way to characterize CH(X).
Proposition A3.4. For any set X,


                 CH(X)={y e R’^ : y =^a^x^, where x^ e X and
                          a ^ G [0,1],f = 1,..., n,and fr + ... a = !}•


In words, the convex hull of X consists of all points that can be written as finite
convex combinations of points in X.

Proof. As an instant corollary of Proposition A3.2, we know that for any convex set
Y that contains X,every finite convex combination of elements contained in X is
an element of Y. So the set on the right-hand side of the equation in the proposition
is certainly in the intersection of all convex sets containing X;that is, it is a subset of
CH(X). But it is easy to see that the set on the right-hand side is itself a convex set:
The convex combination of any two (finite) convex combinations of members of X
is itself a convex combination of members of X. Hence the set on the right-hand
side of the equation is a convex set containing X,and as CH(X) is the intersection
of all such sets, it contains CH(X).                                                     ■
454                                                         Appendix Three: Convexity

Caratheodory's Theorem
The following extension of Proposifion A3.4 is quite useful.
Proposition A3.5 (Caratheodory's Theorem). If X C R'^, any point in CH(A) can
written as a convex combination of no more than k + 1 elements of X.
A mental picture is easy to construct for fc = 2. Take any number of points scattered
in the plane. Their convex hull forms a convex polygon. Think of labeling the
vertices(moving clockwise,say, around the polygon)as vq, Vi,..., Wn • Now picture
the triangles formed by Vq,Vi, and vi) by vq,V2, and V3; by vo,r>3, and Vi, and so
forth. We take wq and each pair of (other) adjacent vertices. These triangles cover
the polygon;each point in the polygon,being in one of the triangles, can be written
as a convex combination of the vertices of "its" triangle. That, for fc = 2, is the
content of Caratheodory's Theorem.
Proof    Fix a set X and a point y e CH(A). By Proposition A3.4, we know that
y can be written as a (finite) convex combination of points in A. It may take 100
points from X,or 1000, but some number n of points from X suffices. Therefore,
there is some least number of points from X that suffices. That is, there is some n
such that y can be written as a convex combination of n points from X but not as
a convex combination of fewer than n.
      Fix n and fix one of the convex-combination representations of y,




where the a* are all strictly greater than zero (or else we could reduce n), less or
equal to one, and sum to one, and the x* are elements of X.
     Suppose that n > k + 1. The set of vectors          for £ = 2,...,7t is a set of at
least k + 1 vectors in ,so it is a linearly dependent set; we can find scalars 7^,
£ = 2,..., n, not all zero, such that


                                 ^7^(x^ - x^)= 0.
                                  ^=2




Define 7' = -                             rewritten


                                        x;7v=o,

where        7^=0. Since the 7^ sum to zero and they are not all zero, at least one
is strictly positive. But now

                                         P P
                                        ax
                                               - A^7^x^,
A3.1. Convex Sets                                                                            455


for any A > 0, because the second summation is zero. Rewrite this as

                                           n



                                      y=           - AyOxh


And now, increase A, starting from zero. At A = 0, the coefficients are all strictly
positive and sum to one, and as you increase A, the coefficients still sum to one
(since the 7^ sum to zero). As A increases, some of the coefficients increase(when
7^ < 0) and some decrease, but since at least one 7^ is strictly positive, there is a
minimum value of A where some                  - Ay^ hits zero. For that value of A, we have
written y as a convex combination of n — 1 of the                     s (or fewer, if two or more
Q,i _       l;ut zero simultaneously), contradicting the minimality of n.
        Hence every y G CH(A) can be written as a convex combination of no more
than fc + 1 of the members of X,which is the result sought.                                    ■


Convexity and closedness combined
We put Caratheodory's Theorem to work to prove the following result.
Proposition A3.6. If X C R'^ is a compact set, then CH(Al) is compact.
Proof. The boundedness of CH(X) is easy: The characterization of CH(X) given
in Proposition A3.4 shows that any bounding set for X effectively bounds CH(X).
(You need to work out how a uniform bound on, say, the norm of elements of a
set bounds the norm of convex combinations of elements of that set.) To show that
CH(Af) is closed, we use Caratheodory: Suppose {x"} is a sequence of points in
                                  n
CH(A) with limit x. Each X can be written as a convex combination of no more
than k + 1 elements out of X. Let me write this as


                                                + ... an,k+l X
                                                             ^n^k+1




Since X is compact(and so is the unit interval,from which all the scalars are drawn),
we can extract a subsequence along which a         Oi  G [0,1] and x^'^          GX
for £ = 1,..., A; + 1, and then

                         X = lim x' =               + ... a     X




Of course, as we pass to the limit,               a*,e = 1, so we conclude that x is indeed
in CH(X).

      In this proof, the argument that the convex hull of a bounded set is bounded
doesn't require that the set is closed. But the argument that the convex hull is closed
relied on the compactness of the set X,to extract those convergent subsequences.
It is not true, in general, that the convex hull of a closed (and unbounded) set is
456                                                       Appendix Three: Convexity

closed. For example,consider the set {x = {xi,X2) e : xi ^ 0,X2 > l/|a:i|}. Draw
the picture if you don't see this in your mind's eye; we have two hyperbolae,one in
the positive orthant and the other in the quadrant where xi < 0 and X2 > 0. This
is indeed a closed set. But its convex hull is the set {x = (xi,X2) e    : X2 > 0},
which is not closed.
     This means that in constructing CH(X), it doesn't work (always) to take the
convex hull of the closure of X. For X as in the example just given, CH(X)is the
set of points where X2 > 0, whereas the convex hull of the closure of X omits the
x-axis. However:


Proposition A3.7. The closure ofa convex set X is convex. Hence, the closure of CH{X)
isCHiX).

Proof. Suppose X is convex. Suppose x and y are in the closure of X. That is,
X = lim„ x" and y = limy" for some sequences {x"} and {y"} from X. Take any
a G [0,1]: For each n, z = ax +(1 - o:)y" G X,because X is convex and, passing
to the limit, we see that lim 2: = lim„(ax" + (1 - a)y") = ax +(1 - a)y is in the
closure of X. The closure of X is indeed convex.
    Since CHCA) is the intersection of all convex sets containing X, CH(X) is in
any convex set that contains X;hence it is in the intersection of all closed and con
vex sets that contain X. That is, CH(JA) C CH(X). Since CH(X)is a closed set, it
is one of fhe sefs whose intersection forms the closure of CH(x),and hence CH(X)
confains the closure of CH(x). But the closure of CH(X) is closed (of course) and
convex (by the first part of this proposition), so the closure of CH(X) is one of the
sets over which an intersection is taken, to form CH(X). Thaf is CH(x) is a subset
of the closure of CH(X). The fwo halves of this paragraph together show that
CH(X) is the closure of CH(X).                                                      ■

     If mixing together convex hulls and closures makes your head spin, just wait
for Chapter 9, where another layer of complication is added.

Extreme points and the Krein-Milman Theorem
Definition A3.8. Tor aset X, x G X is an extreme point of X if x cannot be written
as the convex combination ofsome other pointsfrom X.
Theorem A3.9 (The Krein-Milman Theorem). A compact and convex set X is the
convex hull ofits extreme points.
Krein-Milman holds, in fact, for well-behaved, infinite-dimensional spaces. Its
proof requires the heavy machinery of the axiom of choice, so I won't give a proof.
(See, for instance, Royden, Real Analysis, 1968)
    While I will not give the proof, I do want to point out a corollary to the com
bination of Caratheodory and Krein-Milman. Suppose X <1 Rf \s compact. Then
CH(A)is compact(Proposition A3.6) and convex, so every point can be written as
a convex combination of its extreme points. But it is easy to see that no point in
CH(X) that is not in X is an extreme point, so we can amend this to read: Every
A3.2. The Separating- and Supporting-Hyperplane Theorems                                                   457


point in CH(X) can be written as a convex combination of the extreme points of
X. That is, if X is compact, CH(X) is the convex hull of the extreme points of X.
And then Caratheodory tells us: If X is compact, every point in CH(X) can be written
as a convex combination of no more than k + \ extreme points in X.


A3.2. The Separating- and Supporting-
           Hyperplane Theorems
The Separating- and Supporting-Hyperplane Theorems are some of the most im
portant mathematical hammers in microeconomic theory. The term hyperplane may
need a definition: We are working in i?*, and a hyperplane in i?'' is, for a e ,
a f 0, and a scalar 6, the set of points {x e R'^ : a ■ x = b}. In words, it is almost
a subspace of dimension k -1, where I say "almost" because it is translated away
from the origin by the scalar term b.

Proposition A3.10(The Separating-Hyperplane Theorem). Suppose X and Y are
two disjoint and nonempty'^ convex subsets of R’^; that is, A n F = 0. Then there exist a
nonzero vector a G R'^ and a scalar b G R such that


                        a ■ X > bfor all x G X, and a ■ y < bfor all y GY.

We could equivalently have written the consequent as There exist a (nonzero) linear
function f : R^           R and a constant b such that <p{x) > b for all x G X and 4>(y) < b
for all y gY. Or, also equivalently, There exists a(nonzero)affinefunction f: R’^ ^ R
such that fix) > 0 for all x G X and f{y) < 0 for all y G Y (We mention these
 alternatives, because sometimes you will see the Separating-Hyperplane Theorem
 written these ways.)^
      I will not prove this result(or the two variations to follow). But I briefly discuss
proofs after stating all three.
      For k = 2, hyperplanes are straight lines, and then Figure A3.2 captures what
is going on.
      Proposition A3.10 is squishy on one point. The theorem only guarantees the
weak inequalities a-x >b lor x G X and a-y < b for y gY. The sets X and F can
both intersect the hyperplane. Indeed, in special cases both sets could lie entirely
within the same "separating" hyperplane. We'd like to do better, sharpening the
result so that a ■ x > b for all x G X and a ■ y < b for all y G Y.

      As in Appendix 2, we have a qualifier about sets being nonempty. In this case, we are guarding
against the possibility that X =       and V = 0.
   ^ A function (f> :      —> R is linear if it satisfies <f>{x + y)= 4>(x)+ (f>(y). As you doubtless know from
linear algebra, in J?*, every linear function has the form (j>(x) = a ■ x for some a e R^ ■ A function is
affine if it has the form a ■ x + b,(or a e R'^ and b e R.
   ^ Indeed, if you ever see this generalized to infinite-dimensional spaces, the version that       concerns

linearfunctions is the most often observed form, and there will be some concern over whether the linear
function in question can be assumed to be continuous. In this context, the Separating-Hyperplane The
orem is typically derived as a corollary to the Hahn-Banach Theorem.
458                                                            Appendix Three: Convexity




                                                                     (b)

       Figure A3.2. Separating hyperpianes. In panei a, the two disjoint convex sets are
       separated by a hyperpiane. In panel b, the two sets are disjoint, but one is not
       convex, and there is no hyperpiane that wiii separate them.


       This may not be possible: For general convex disjoint sets X and Y,it may be
necessary that both inequalities are allowed to be weak. Suppose,for example,that
k = 2, X = {(xi,X2) : Xi < 0 or xi = 0 and X2 > 0}, and let Y be the complement of
X.(Draw the picture, if this isn't clear to you.) And even if the sets are both closed,
it may be necessary that at least one of the inequalities is weak. For instance, let
k = 1, X = {{xi^xi) : X] < 0}, and y = {(xi,X2) : X2 > 1/xi, xi > 0}.) However:

Proposition A3.11(The Strict-Separation Theorem). Suppose X and Y are closed,
 nonempty, and disjoint convex subsets of . If either X or Y is compact, then we can
find a nonzero vector a e.   and a scalar b such that a-x > b for all x e X and a-y < b
for all y eY.

       For a third variation, we need a definition.


Definition A3.12. A point x e R’^ is a boundary point of X C R'^ if x is in both the
closure of X and the closure of the complement of X.
Note that we do not require that x is an element oi X. An alternative definition
would be that x is a boundary point of X if we can find sequences {x*} and {y*},
where x* e X and y® ^ X for all i, such that both sequences have x as limit. Of
course, the trivial sequence x,x,... serves as either the first or the second of these,
depending on whether or not x e X.

Proposition A3.13(The Supporting-Hyperplane Theorem). Suppose X is a convex
subset of R^ and x° is a boundary point of X. Then there exist nonzero a £ R^ and
b £ R such that a - x° = b and a ■ x < b for all x £ X.

This is called the Supporting-Hyperplane Theorem because of pictures such as
Figure A3.3: The hyperpiane through x° "supports"(roughly, is tangent to) X at
,x“.

       We do not provide proofs of any of these results, but a few remarks about
 proofs may be helpful.
A3.3. The Support-Function Theorem                                                    459




      Figure A3.3. A supporting hyperpiane. The point x”, on the boundary of the convex
      set, is supported by the hyperpiane shown.


  1. Separating one convex set from another is really no harder than separating a
     convex set that doesn't contain the origin from the origin with a linear function;
     if X and Y are disjoint convex sets that we wish to separate, we can recast the
     problem as requiring the separation of 0 from the sei X -Y \= [z e           \ z=
     X - y,x e X,y e Y}. For if a e i?'' is such that a ■ z > Q iox &\\ z e X - Y,
     then infjjgx a ■ x must be greater or equal to sup^^y a - y-
  2. Moreover, if X and Y are both closed, and one is compact, it is not hard to
     show that X - y is closed,so strict separation comes down to strict separation
     of the origin from a closed convex set Z that doesn't contain the origin. Put
     a ball of radius e around the origin, where e is chosen so that the ball doesn't
     intersect Z. Such balls are convex(why?),so apply the Separating-Hyperplane
     Theorem to the ball and Z.

 3. And in the Supporting-Flyperplane Theorem,two cases need to be considered.
    U X ^ X, then this involves separating x from X; it is immediate from the
    Separating-Hyperplane Theorem. While if x G X, we separate X from each
    in a sequence of points that approaches x from outside of X and pass to a
     limit along a subsequence.


A3.3. The Support-Function Theorem
A hyperpiane {x &          \ a-x = b} defines two closed half-spaces,{x £ R’^ ■. a-x >b]
and {x e R*" a-x <b]. The Strict-Separation Theorem then tells us the following.
Proposition A3.14. Every closed and convex set X except for R’^ itself consists of the
intersection of all the closed half-spaces that contain it.
Proof Fix X. li X f R'‘, some point tf lies in i?'' \ X. The Strict-Separation
Theorem tells us that there is some a e R^ and b such that a ■ x > b tor all x e X
and a ■ y° <b. Therefore, at least one closed half-space contains X. Of course, the
intersection of all closed half-spaces that contain X contains X. And if y is not
in X, the Strict-Separation Theorem can be used once again to find a closed half
space that contains X but not y. So the intersection of all the closed half-spaces
containing X contains no point not in X. (The proposition is true for the case of
460                                                                     Appendix Three: Convexity

X = %, but the proof just given doesn't quite work. Why not? And can you create
a special argument for this special case?)                                    ■

      Since the intersection of closed and convex sets is closed and convex, this
provides us with another characterization of a closed and convex set (albeit not
one that is useful): A set in           is closed and convex if and only if if is             itself or
is the intersection of a collection of closed half-spaces. Also, this tells us that for
any set X, CH(X)is either R^ or the intersection of all the closed half-spaces that
contain X. (If it is not R’^, CH(A) is the intersection of all the closed half-spaces
that contain CH(A), but each of these perforce also contains X. And no closed
half-space that contains X omits any point from CH(A),since a closed half-space
is closed and convex, and CH(A) is the intersection of all closed and convex sets
containing X.)
      One way to restate Proposition A3.14 begins with the following definition. For
each closed and convex set X and for each a & R^, define


                                   <t>x(o) := sup{a ■ x : x G A},

where we define <j>x{o) = oo if sup               a ■ X is infinife.^ I claim that


                         <Pxia) = inf{6 G R : a ■ x < b for all x G X},

where the infimum equals oo if the set of such b's is empty. To show that these two
are the same,first look at the case where the supremum is unbounded. This means
that for every b, there is some x G X such that a - x > b, and so the set over which
the infimum is taken is empty. On the other hand, if the supremum is finite, then
for any b fhat is greater than or equal to the supremum, a ■ x < b ior all x G X,
and hence b makes it into the set over which we are taking the infimum, while if b
is less than the supremum,then there is some x G X with a-x> b, and b is not in
the set of the infimum. Therefore, fhe infimum is precisely fhe supremum.

abovefor closed and convex sets X and a G R^,

Proof We know from Proposition A3.14 that X is either i?'' or it is the intersection
of all the closed half-spaces that contain it. If A = R^, then it is easy to see that

    Suppose {x ■. a ■ X <b) is a closed half-space containing A. That is, a • x < 6

see in the book, you can prove that the support function as defined here is convex and
homogeneous in a, making due allowance for the possibility of infinife values of f.

A3.4. Concave and Convex Functions

A3.5. Quasi-concavity and Quasi-convexity
In microeconomic theory, the property of concavity given in Proposition A3.17(d),
that sets {x G A : /(x) > r} are convex for r e i?, is very important. This property
comes up in at least two places, as a property of consumer's preferences (leading
to nicely behaved utility representations), and for ifs consequences in terms of
constrained-maximization problems. To see the first, you should consult Chapter
2 in the text; the second is given (soon) in Proposition A3.21. But granting the
significance of this property, it is natural to ask. Does this property characterize
464                                                                    Appendix Three: Convexity

concave functions, or is there a broader class of functions with this property? The
latter is true.


Definition A3.18. Suppose f : X              R where X is a convex subset of           .
  a. Thefunction f is quasi-concave if f(ax +(1 — a)y) > f{y)for all x,y e X such
     that f{x) > fiy) andfor all a G [0,1].
  b. Thefunction / is strictly quasi-concave if f{ax +{l-a)y) > f{y)forall x,y e X,
     xfy,such that f(x) > f{y) and for all a G (0,1).
  c. Thefunction f is semi-strictly quasi-concave if it is quasi-concave and if f{ax +
     (1 - a)y) > fiy),for all x and y with f{x) > fiy) andfor all a G (0,1).

     that fix) > fiy) andfor all a G [0,1].
  e. Thefunction f is strictly quasi-convex if fiax +(1 - a)y) < fix)for x and y,
     x f y, such that fix) > fiy) and for all a G (0,1).^

Proof Suppose / is concave, and (x,r) and (x', r') are two points in the hypograph

Proposition A3.26. Suppose f : X          R is homogeneous ofdegree l,for a domain X C
    that is a cone (possibly lacking the vertex 0). If f is a supergradient (or subgradient)
of f at the point y, then f is a linearfunction. That is, 4>(x) = a - x for some a G .

Proof.  We know that <p{x) = a ■ x + b (or a G R^ and b G R; we need to

a ■ 2y + b > f{2y) = 2f{y) = 2{a ■ y + b) = a ■ 2y + 2b. Hence 0 > 5. Repeat the
argument with 0.5y in place of 2y, and you get .55 > 0. This implies 5 = 0. The
same argument works(with signs reversed)for subgradients.                      ■



3.7. Concave and Convex Functions and Calculus
In Chapter 12, the following result will be needed:

Proposition A3.27. Suppose f is a concave (respectively, convex)function of one real
variable, with domain the open interval (a,5). Then f has a left- and right-hand derivative
at every point x G (a, b). The left- and right-hand derivatives off are both nonincreasing
(resp., nondecreasing)functions, the left-hand derivative at x is greater or equal (resp.,
less or equal) to the right-hand derivative at x, and if x > x', the right-hand derivative
at x! is greater or equal (resp., less or equal) to the left-hand derivative at x. The left- and
right-hand derivatives are equal to one another at all but (at most) a countable number of
points in (a, b). And, over any closed subinterval of(a, b), f(b)- /(a) is the integral of
the "derivative" of f, where one can usefor the integrand any value desired at the (at most)
countable number of points at which the right- and left-hand derivatives do not agree.
In other words, / has a derivative at all but a countable number of points, and the
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus holds for /: It is the integral of its derivative.
Most of this proposition is relatively straightforward to prove, if you know about
the concept of absolute continuity and its connection to indefinite integration. See,
for instance, Royden (1968).^
     Finally, in several places in the book we use the following characterization of
concavity (and convexity).
Proposition A3.28. Suppose f is a twice-continuously differentiable function from a
convex and open domain of R^ to R. Then f is concave if and only if the matrix of
its mixed second partial derivatives (its Hessian matrix) is negative semi-definite, when
evaluated at all points in domain of f. It is strictly concave if and only if its Hessian
matrix is negative definite everywhere. For the case k = 1, this is: f is concave if and
only if f", its second derivative, is nonpositive, and it is strictly concave if and only if f"
is strictly negative. (For convex functions, change negative to positive and nonpositive to
nonegative.)



   ^ I am told that the third edition (and perhaps subsequent editions) of Royden doesn't give this
result in its entirety. I'm relying on the second edition here.
Appendix Four

Correspondences
In economics, problems frequently have more than one "answer" at a time. This
"answer" can be the solution of a constrained optimization problem or the equilib
rium of an economy, a market, or a game. Whatever it is, we need mathematical
language for sets of answers/solutions/equilibria that change with changes in the
parameters of the situation. The mathematical concept used in this case is a corre
spondence. This appendix introduces you to this concept and to important associated
concepts, definitions, and results.

Marshallian demand

For purposes of illustration, we carry along two examples. The first comes from
Chapter 3. A consumer's preferences are given by a continuous utility function
u : R!1 ^ R,and for strictly positive prices p e     and a nonnegative income level
y, the consumer's problem is

                    Maximize u{x), subject to p ■ x < y,x > 0.

Per Proposition 3.1, continuity of ensures that the problem has a solution (you
are maximizing a continuous function over a compact set), the set of solutions is
convex if u is quasi-concave, and the problem has a single solution if u is strictly
quasi-concave. We do not generally assume strict quasi-concavity; hence there
may be more than one solution. For given p and y, we write D(p, y) for the set of
solutions of the problem, and we write I'ip, y) for the value of the optimal solution;
that is, it X & D(p, y), then v{p,y) = u(x). As you learn in Chapter 3, D(p,y) is
called Marshallian demand at the prices p and income level y, and n(p, y) is called
the indirect utility at that point.

Nash equilibria of a finite game
The second example comesfrom Volume 2,but it is simple and basic enough so that
many readers will have seen it already. (If you've never seen anything like this, you
may wish to skip this example.) Fix positive integers m and n, and imagine two
mxn matrices of real numbers, A and B. Let e'" denote the m-dimensional vector
all of whose components are 1, and let e" be the n-dimensional vector of this sort.
Let5 = {s e R^:s> 0,,s-e™ = 1},andlet T = {t G i?” ; Ce" = 1}. That is, 5 is the
space of probability distributions with m components or outcomes, and T is the
space of probability distributions with n components or outcomes. We write sAt
and sDt for the obvious scalar-vector-scalar products, thinking of t as a column
470                                                  Appendix Four: Correspondences

vector for this purpose. Note that the dimensions are such that sAt and sBt are
both scalars. Then for given A and B,we say that (s,t) is a Nash equilibrium for the
bimatrix game {A,B) if sAt = sup {s'At : s' G 5} and sBt = sup {sBt' : t' G T}.
For a given {A,B), there is no obvious reason to suppose that some pair (s,t)
forms a Nash equilibrium for {A,B), but in fact a very nice application of fixed-
point theory shows that this is so. For for any {A,B),let E(A,B)be the set of (s,t)
that are Nash equilibria for {A,B).


A4.1. Functions and Correspondences
A function is a mathematical object that associates to every point in one set (the
domain of the function) a single point in a second set(the range). The definition of a
function specifies that each point in the domain is mapped into a single point in the
range. (This is, two points in the domain may be mapped into the same point in the
range; but a single point in the domain can't take on two different range values.)
    A correspondence generalizes the idea of a function, allowing a point in the
domain to be associated with more (or less) than one point in the range. Given
sets X and Y, which are called the domain and range of the correspondence, a
correspondence 0 is a map that associates to every point x e X a subset fix) of Y.
     In general,the domain X and range y of a correspondence can be very general
mathematical objects. In our work, however, X will always be a subset of i?" for
some n,and Y will always be a closed subset of R™', for some m. Accordingly,for
all the applications we discuss, we can take limits of sequences of points in X or
in Y, measure (Euclidean) distances between points in either set, and so on.
     Note that a correspondence is nothing more than a fancy function, in the fol
lowing sense. Let Y denote the set of all subsets of Y,then the correspondence f is
a function with domain X and range Y. But to emphasize that the targets are sets,
the terminology correspondence is used. To write symbolically that f isa correspon
dence from A to y —that is, (p{x) C Y for each x G X—we write <f : X =^Y and
X => 4>{x), using a double arrow. (In comparison, f : X ^ Y is used to indicate
that (p is a function from X to y.)

     In the example of Marshallian demand, two correspondences are worthy of
attention. The more obvious, perhaps, is the correspondence that maps price-
income pairs (p, y) into the consumer's Marshallian demand, or D :        x    ^
R{. But there is also the budget set correspondence that defines, for each p and
y, the consumer's feasible set: If B{p,y) denotes [x G R'{ : p ■ x < y}, then
B : R’p., X   =t- R{: is a second correspondence.
     In the example ofequilibria of bimatrix games, E : R
                                                          mxn
                                                            Xjjmxn ^ R^xRI'is
the equilibrium correspondence; E(A,B)is the set of (s,t) that are Nash equilibria
for (A,B). Note that (s,t) actually come from a smaller space than the range
Rf X R'p written here; they are probability distributions, whose components are
both nonnegative (which is indicated) and sum to 1 (which is not).

      Language commonly used concerning correspondences includes:
A4.2. Continuity of Correspondences                                                   471


  1. If the correspondence </> never maps a point in the domain into the empty set,
     we say that (j) is nonempty valued. So,for example,because u is continuous and
     the budget sets are compact, D is nonempty valued; because of a fixed-point
     theorem, E is nonempty valued.
  2. If (pix) is convex for every x e X, we say that (p is convex valued. So, if u
     is quasi-concave, D is convex valued. But (for those who know a smattering
     of game theory), because of games such as the Battle of the Sexes, E is most
     definitely not convex valued.
  3. If the correspondence (p maps every point in the domain into a set containing
     a single element, we say that <p is singleton valued. Therefore, if u is strictly
     quasi-concave, the Marshallian demand correspondence is singleton valued.
  4. If (p{x) is closed for every x e X, we say that (p is closed valued. If (p{x) is
     compact for every x e X,we say that (p is compact valued. As in: as part of the
     proof that D is nonempty valued, one must prove that B is a compact-valued
     correspondence. Or,since one can prove that the set of equilibria for any game
     is a closed set, and since equilibria(being pairs of probability distributions)are
     clearly bounded, E is compact valued.
  5. The graph of the correspondence 0 : JT            F is the set of points {(x,y) e
    XxY : ye 4>(x)}.

     A property of correspondences that we should spell out more formally is given
in the following definition.
Definition A4.1. The correspondence <p is locally bounded iffor every x e X, there
exists an e{x) > 0 and a bounded set Y{x) CY such that fix') C Y{x)for all x' that
are less than e distantfrom x.

To paraphrase,the range Y may be unbounded but,for every point x in the domain,
we can simultaneously bound (p{x') for all x' in some (small) neighborhood of x.

and convex-valued,and whose graph is a closed set, but that is not locally bounded.

A4.2. Continuity of Correspondences
In parallel with the idea of continuity of a function,correspondences have continuity
properties. Specifically, three notions of continuity are important; upper semi
continuity, lower semi-continuity and (full) continuity. Definitions vary from book
to book, but for our purposes, the following will do.
Definition A4.2.

 a. The correspondence cp : X       Y is upper semi-continuous if, whenever {xn} is a
    sequence in X with limit x e X,and {y„} is a sequence in Y such that          G (p{x„)
    for all n and limn Un exists, then this limit point is an element of cp{x).
472                                                               Appendix Four: Correspondences

  b. The correspondence <p : X ^ Y is lower semi-continuous iffor every x e X,
     sequence {xn} from X with limit x, and y G fix), we can find,for all n > N for
     some sufficiently large N, e f{xn) such that limn Vn = V-
  c. The correspondence f : X ^ Y is continuous if it is both upper and lower semi-
      continuous.



The meaning of upper semi-continuity can be partially divined from fhe following
alternative characterization.


Proposition A4.3. The correspondence f : X ^Y is upper semi-continuous ifand only
if the graph of f is (relatively) closed in X xY.

Proof. This is less of a proposition than a remark,since it is just a matter of recalling
the "sequential convergence" definition of a(relatively)closed set. f is upper semi-
continuous per Definition A4.2a, and          y„)} is a sequence from the graph of f
with limit (x,y). Since {x„} has limit x. Definition A4.2a implies that y G fix),
holds. This is just another way of saying that (x,y) is in the graph of f. Therefore,
the graph of is a closed set. On the other hand,suppose the graph of is a closed
set and that lim„ x„ = x, lim„              = y, and        G fixn) for each n. Then {(x„, y„)}
is a sequence from the graph of f with limit point ix,y), and since the graph of f
is closed, this means that (x, y) is in the graph, which is just to say that y G fix).
      The only part of this that requires more than a moment's thought concerns
the parenthetical relatively in the statement of the proposition. Because X is not
necessarily a closed set, when we say that the graph of f is closed, we can't mean
closed in the full Euclidean space that contains X xY,but instead we must mean
that it is relatively closed within X xY. That characterization of a relatively closed
set that we've used—that it contains all its limit points within the space—shows
this.                                                                                                  ■


        Here is another very simple result.

Proposition A4.4. If a correspondence f is upper semi-continuous, then fix) is closed
for each x. Therefore, ifa correspondence f is upper semi-continuous and locally bounded,
it is compact valued.

Proof. If f is upper semi-continuous, its graph is closed. Since the intersection of
two closed sets is closed, and {x} x D is a closed set, the intersection of the graph
of f and {x} x Y,or {x} x fix), is closed. Therefore, fix) is closed.^ And if fhe
correspondence is, in addition, locally bounded,then it is bounded at each x and,
therefore, compact valued.                                                                             ■

        Some examples with pictures may help you understand these definitions.

        Since Y is always a closed set, we don't need in this case to worry about relative closures.
A4.2. Continuity of Correspondences                                                  473



  1. Figure A4.1(a)shows the correspondence 0 defined for X = Y = R as follows:

                                {4-x,2       x} for X < 2,

        This correspondence is upper semi-continuous. Perhaps the easiest way to
   see this is that its graph is a closed set. But it is not at all lower semi-continuous.
   It fails at X = 2 and at x = 3. For instance, 1 e <p(2). But as you approach
   X = 1 from below, say along the sequence in which x„ = 2 — 1/n, you can't get
   "close" to the value 1; you can find a sequence approaching the value y = 2 and
   another approaching y = 0, but y = 1 can't be approached.
2. Now consider the correspondence <p' :           R defined by

                              f {4 - X,2- x} for X < 2,
                     (/>'(x) = •! [3- X,5- x] for 2 < X < 3, and
                                {x-3}             for X > 3.


   This is graphed for you in Figure A4.1b; the open circle indicates a point that
   is not part of the correspondence. This is just like the previous example,except
   that for domain values x S [2,3], the correspondence has been "shifted up."
   This is not an upper semi-continuous correspondence; It should be clear to you
   that the graph is not closed; it lacks the accumulation point (2,0). (If it had
   that point, the graph would be closed and the correspondence would be upper
   semi-continuous.) It also fails to be lower semi-continuous, both at x = 2 and
   at X = 3. But imagine changing it at x = 2 and x = 3 as follows: Suppose the
   correspondence had 0(2) = 0 and 0(3) = {0}. Then it would be lower semi-
   continuous. It is lower semi-continuous at x = 2 trivially; since 0(2) = 0, the
   definition has no bite there. And at x = 3, no matter how you approach x,
   there are values in the correspondence along the sequence that approach the
   one point,0, in the correspondence at the limit.

A4.3. Singleton-valued Correspondences and Continuity
For reasons that will become apparent next section, the case of singleton-valued
correspondences is of special interest. Throughout this section, we deal with a
function f : X          and a correspondence (j): X ^ Y where (p{x) = {f(x)}. We say
that <f) describes the function /,/ is the function associated with the correspondence
(p, and (p is the (singleton-valued) correspondence associated with the function /.

Proposition A4.5. A singleton-valued correspondence (p is lower semi-continuous ifand
only if it describes a continuousfunction, in which case it is also upper semi-continuous.

Proof. Suppose (p is lower semi-continuous. Take any x € X and sequence
{xn} with limit x. Since f{x) G (p{x), lower semi-continuity says that, for some
sequences {y„} with ?/„ G 4>{xn), lim„j/„ = f{x). But the only choice for      is
f{xn). Therefore, / is continuous. Conversely, for x G X and {x„} with limit
X, continuity of / ensures that lim„/(x„) = /(x). Therefore, for all y G (p{x),
and there is but one such y, namely /(x), we can find     G <p{xn), namely /(x„),
with limit /(x). This is lower semi-continuity. And, in either case, for any {x„}
with limit x, if   G <p{xn), then   = f{xn), and the (only) accumulation point of
yn = lim„ /(Xn)= fix) G (p(x), which is upper semi-continuity.                  ■

    On the other hand, in this special case of singleton-valued correspondences,
upper semi-continuity does not ensure continuity of the corresponding function.
Consider X = [0,oo) and /(x) = 1/x for x > 0 and /(O)= 0. This function is very
discontinuous at x = 0, but the singleton-valued correspondence associated with
this function is upper semi-continuous.
A4.4. Parametric Optimization Problems and Berge's Theorem                           475


    This example shows a sort of behavior we will want to avoid in applications,
namely, where the function/correspondence "disappears" over the horizon as you
get close to some value of x and then pops up at that x somewhere completely
different. The definition we gave earlier of a locally bounded correspondence is
just what is needed.

Proposition A4.6. A singleton-valued correspondence (j) that is locally bounded is upper
semi-continuous if and only if it is lower semi-continuous if and only if the function it
describes is continuous.


Proof Suppose <p is locally bounded and singleton valued. Let f{x) denote the
single element of fix), for each x. If / is continuous, then f is clearly upper
semi-continuous: If    —> x, j/n e 0(x„) for each n, and     y, then    = /(x„)
and by continuity of /, y = lim„ = lim„ /(x„) = /(x), so y e f{x) = {/(x)}.
     On the other hand, suppose f is upper semi-continuous. Take any sequence
x,     X. Since f is locally bounded, there is a bounded set y and an e > 0 such
that fix') G r if|
                 |x'- x|| < e. But ||x, — X < e for all by finifely many x„ (since
X,
         x), which implies that the set {/(x„);n = 1,2,...} is a bounded set. This
implies that every subsequence of the sequence {/(x„)} has a further subsequence
that converges to some y. But this y must be in f{x) by the upper semi-continuity
of (f). That is, y = /(x). So,every subsequence of {/(x„)} has a further subsequence
that converges to /(x), which implies that the original sequence {/(x„)} converges
to fix). This makes / continuous.
    This shows that if f is locally bounded and singleton valued, upper semi-
confinuify of f is equivalent to continuity of the corresponding function /. Propo
sition A4.5 shows then that upper semi-continuity is equivalent to lower semi
continuity, since lower semi-continuity is equivalent to continuity of /.         ■


A4.4. Parametric Constrained Optimization Problems
     and Berge's Theorem
A constrained optimization problem takes the form
      Maximize [or minimize]some objectivefunction, subject to some constraints.
A parametric constrained optimization problem is a family of constrained opti
mization problems, where either the objective function or the constraint set (or
both)varies parametrically. In what follows, we deal with maximization problems;
everything we say works for minimization problems.^
    We imagine that the parameter, denoted by 9, comes from a subset of i?” for
some n, and the variables that are optimized, denoted by z, come from a subset of
R"' for some m. The objective function is written Fiz,9), and for each 9, the set
of feasible 2 values is denoted by Ai9). In notation, then, a parametric constrained
maximization problem is written

 ^ Mininimizing / is the same as maximizing —/,so this is almost immediate.
476                                                              Appendix Four: Correspondences

                For each 6, maximize (in z) F(z,9), subject to 2 G A{6).
For a parametric constrained maximization problem of this form, we let Z{9) be
the set of solutions of the problem for parameter value 9—that is, Z(-) is a cor
respondence with argument 9, giving (as values) sets of 2—and we let f{9) =
sup {F{z,9)-,z e A{9)}A
    An excellent example of a parametric constrained maximization problem is the
consumer's problem(CP).The parameter 9 is the vector of prices and income ip,Y),
and the (vector) variable 2 is the consumption bundle x chosen by the consumer.
The correspondence of solutions is the Marshallian demand correspondence, and
the function / is the indirect utility function. Note that in this case, the objective
function u{x) is independent of the parameters (p, y), but the constraint set for x,
B(p, y), does change with changes in (p, y).
    Economic theory repeatedly uses the following proposition. The version given
here is a bit "fancier" than is standard; see the comments following the statement
of the corollary.

Proposition A4.7(Berge's Theorem,also known as the Theorem of the Maximum).
Consider the parametric constrained-maximization problem


                            Maximize F{z,9), subject to z G A{9).


Let Z(9) be the set of solutions of this problem for the parameter 9, and let f{9) =
sup {F{z,9);z e A(9)}. If
 a. F is a continuousfunction in {z,9),
 b. 9 ^ A(9) is lower semi-continuous, and

 c. there exists for each 9 a set B{9) C A{9) such that Z(9) C B{9), sup{F(z,6): z G
    B{9)} = sup{F{z,9) ■. z G A(9)}, and 9        B{9) is an upper semi-continuous and
    locally bounded correspondence^
Then:

 d. Z{9) is nonempty for all 9, and 9              Z{9) is an upper semi-continuous and locally
      bounded correspondence; and
 e. the function 9        f{9) is continuous.
Identical conclusions hold if the optimization problem calls for minimizing F rather than
maximizing F.

  ^ We write sup instead of max in the definition of / to cover the case where there is no x e A{9) that
is optimal.
      We could, in theory, consider a parametric constrained-maximization problem in which, for some
values of 0, A(9) = 0. For those values of 9, Z(9) would be empty and f(9) would be —00. But
we have just ruled this out, at least as far as this result is concerned. Please note that this assumption
doesn't establish that Z(9) is nonempty; we'll need to prove this. (It isn't hard.) But it does establish
that f(9)=f —00, because it tells us that A(9) is nonempty.
